title: Intrusive Thought Perspective Worksheet
description: Use this worksheet to practice relating differently to intrusive thoughts. Instead of arguing with or analyzing the thought, you'll compare what OCD says the thought means versus what a compassionate, science-based perspective would say.
instructions: When you notice an intrusive thought that feels distressing, work through each step below. The goal is NOT to make the thought go away — it's to practice a different response. This worksheet is for educational self-reflection and is best used alongside therapy with an OCD-trained clinician.

fields.label: 1. The Intrusive Thought — What thought, image, or urge showed up?
fields.placeholder: e.g., An image of pushing someone, a thought about contamination, a feeling that something terrible will happen if I don't check, a blasphemous thought during prayer

fields.label: 2. What OCD Says It Means — How does OCD interpret this thought?
fields.placeholder: e.g., 'I'm a dangerous person', 'I must secretly want this', 'Something terrible will happen', 'I'm morally corrupt'

fields.label: 3. Distress Level — How distressed do you feel? (0 = not at all, 100 = most distressed ever)
4. OCD Pattern — Which OCD thinking pattern is at work?

options.label: Thought-Action Fusion — 'Thinking it = wanting it or doing it'
Overimportance of Thoughts — 'This thought must mean something significant'
Intolerance of Uncertainty — 'I need to be 100% sure'
Inflated Responsibility — 'If something bad happens, it's my fault'
Perfectionism — 'It has to be exactly right or it's wrong'
Threat Overestimation — 'The worst-case scenario is the most likely'

fields.label: 5. Compassionate Perspective — What would a knowledgeable, compassionate person say about this thought?
fields.placeholder: e.g., 'Almost everyone has intrusive thoughts like this — it's a normal brain event, not a sign of your character. The distress you feel shows how much you care about your values, not that you're dangerous.'

fields.label: 6. Response Choice — What will you do instead of ritualizing? (e.g., label it, 'maybe maybe not', return to your activity)
fields.placeholder: e.g., 'I'm going to label this as an OCD thought and continue what I was doing. Maybe, maybe not — I'm not going to figure this out right now.'

fields.label: 7. What Happened — Were you able to resist the compulsion? What did you notice?
fields.placeholder: e.g., I labeled it and went back to cooking. The anxiety spiked for about 10 minutes then gradually eased. I didn't check or seek reassurance.

fields.label: 8. Distress Now — How distressed do you feel now? (0 = not at all, 100 = most distressed ever)

distortionsList.name: Thought-Action Fusion
distortionsList.description: Believing that having a thought is the same as doing the action, or that thinking about something makes it more likely to happen. In reality, thoughts are mental events — not intentions, desires, or predictions.

distortionsList.name: Overimportance of Thoughts
distortionsList.description: Treating a random intrusive thought as deeply significant or meaningful. In reality, the brain produces thousands of thoughts daily, many of which are random, bizarre, or contrary to your values.

distortionsList.name: Intolerance of Uncertainty
distortionsList.description: Feeling that you must be 100% certain about something or the anxiety is unbearable. OCD demands certainty that is impossible to achieve — learning to tolerate 'maybe' is key to recovery.

distortionsList.name: Inflated Responsibility
distortionsList.description: Believing you are personally responsible for preventing all possible harm, even events outside your control. This drives checking, reassurance-seeking, and excessive caution.

distortionsList.name: Perfectionism
distortionsList.description: Feeling that things must be done exactly right, completely, or perfectly — or something bad will happen. Drives repeating, re-doing, and 'just right' compulsions.

distortionsList.name: Threat Overestimation
distortionsList.description: Overestimating the probability or severity of feared outcomes. OCD makes unlikely events feel inevitable and manageable outcomes feel catastrophic.

disclaimer: This worksheet is for educational self-reflection, not treatment. It helps you practice recognizing OCD patterns. Actual OCD treatment (ERP) should be guided by a trained therapist. If you are having thoughts about ending your life, call or text 988 immediately. If you are in crisis, contact the 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline.
